questions.question:
Three bells P, Q and R are rung periodically in a school. P is rung every 20 minutes; Q is rung every 30 minutes and R is rung every 50 minutes. If all the three bells are rung at 12:00 PM, when will the three bells ring together again the next time?

A. 5:00 PM
B. 5:30 PM
C. 6:00 PM
D. 6:30 PM
questions.to_find: the next time all three bells ring together after 12:00 PM

understand.plain: Three bells ring at intervals of 20, 30, and 50 minutes. They all ring at 12:00 PM. We need to find when they will all ring together again, which happens at the LCM of their intervals. The LCM tells us the smallest time period after which all three return to their common starting point.

keywords.explain: The smallest positive integer divisible by each of the given numbers. For periodic events, the LCM of their periods gives the next simultaneous occurrence.
keywords.example: LCM(4, 6) = 12 because 12 is the smallest number that both 4 and 6 divide evenly.

keywords.explain: Each bell rings at regular fixed intervals, repeating forever. All three will coincide again after exactly LCM of their intervals.
keywords.example: A bell ringing every 6 minutes rings at 0, 6, 12, 18, ... minutes.

keywords.explain: Breaking a number into its prime factors is the standard method for computing LCM. LCM takes the highest power of each prime factor.
keywords.example: 20 = 2^2 x 5, 30 = 2 x 3 x 5, so LCM includes 2^2, 3, 5.

understand.visual_alt: Three bells ringing at 20, 30, and 50 minute intervals, all starting at 12:00 PM.

given.aim: Find the next time all three bells ring simultaneously.

terms.connects: one of the three periods whose LCM we must find
second period contributing to the LCM
third period contributing to the LCM

terms.meaning: All three bells ring at 12:00 PM together
terms.example: This is the reference point; next simultaneous ring is 12:00 PM + LCM minutes
terms.connects: anchor from which we add LCM minutes to find the answer

given.plan: Compute LCM(20, 30, 50) using prime factorization. Then add that many minutes to 12:00 PM to find the next simultaneous ringing.

steps.title: Prime factorize each interval
steps.formula_raw: per prime, lcm takes the maximum exponent
steps.note: Each prime's highest power across all three numbers is selected for the LCM.

steps.title: Compute LCM(20, 30, 50)
steps.formula_raw: LCM = product of highest prime powers
steps.apply: LCM = 2^2 x 3 x 5^2 = 4 x 3 x 25 = 300 minutes.
steps.note: 300 minutes is the smallest interval after which all three bells ring simultaneously.

steps.title: Convert 300 minutes to hours and find the clock time
steps.apply: 300 / 60 = 5 hours. Starting from 12:00 PM, adding 5 hours gives 5:00 PM.
steps.note: 300 minutes = exactly 5 hours, so all bells ring together again at 5:00 PM.

solution.result: All three bells ring together again at 5:00 PM, which is option A.

questions.formula_note: LCM via prime factorization directly gives the next simultaneous event time for periodic bells.

questions.question: The ratio of male to female students in a college for five years is plotted in the following line graph. The graph shows the male-to-female ratio for years 2007 through 2011. In 2007 the ratio is 2.5, and in 2008 the ratio is 3.0. If the number of female students doubled in 2008, by what percent did the number of male students increase in 2008?
questions.to_find: the percentage increase in the number of male students in 2008

understand.plain: A line graph shows the ratio of male to female students over five years. We are told the female population doubled from 2007 to 2008. Using the ratio values from the graph for 2007 and 2008, we can express the male count in both years and compute the percentage increase.

keywords.explain: The ratio R = (number of male students) / (number of female students) at a given year. If R = 2.5 and female count = F, then male count = 2.5F.
keywords.example: If ratio is 3 and female count is 100, then male count is 300.

keywords.explain: Percentage increase = (new value - old value) / old value x 100.
keywords.example: If males go from 250 to 600, increase = (600 - 250)/250 x 100 = 140%.

keywords.explain: The number of female students in 2008 is twice that of 2007. This is the key constraint that lets us connect the two years.
keywords.example: If female count in 2007 was F, then female count in 2008 is 2F.

220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe x1='50' y1='180' x2='330' y2='180' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='30' y='185' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>2007</text><text x='120' y='185' class='sub understand.visual_alt: Line graph showing male-to-female ratio; 2007 is 2.5 and 2008 is 3.0, marking the key values for this problem.

given.aim: Find the percentage increase in male students from 2007 to 2008.

terms.example: If female count in 2007 = F, then male count in 2007 = 2.5F
terms.connects: provides the baseline male count in terms of F

terms.example: If female count in 2008 = 2F (doubled), then male count in 2008 = 3 x 2F = 6F
terms.connects: together with doubled female count, gives the new male count

terms.meaning: Female count in 2008 = 2 x Female count in 2007 = 2F
terms.example: If 2007 had 100 female students, 2008 has 200 female students
terms.connects: key bridge linking 2007 and 2008 male counts through the ratio
final step converting the change in male count to a percentage

given.plan: Let female count in 2007 be F. Express male counts in 2007 and 2008 using the ratios and the doubling condition. Apply the percentage increase formula.

steps.title: Set up male counts using ratio and female data
steps.formula_raw: male count = ratio x female count
steps.apply: Let female count in 2007 = F. Male count in 2007 = R_2007 x F = 2.5F. Female count in 2008 = 2F (given doubled). Male count in 2008 = R_2008 x 2F = 3.0 x 2F = 6F.
steps.note: The ratio from the graph and the doubling condition together determine the exact male counts.

steps.title: Compute percentage increase in male students
steps.note: The F cancels out, so the answer is independent of the actual female count.

solution.result: The number of male students increased by 140% in 2008.

questions.formula_note: No standard formula ID needed; the solution uses the basic ratio relationship and percentage increase definition directly.

questions.question:
The ratio of male to female students in a college for five years is plotted in the following line graph. The graph shows male-to-female ratio: 2008 = 3, 2009 = 2, 2010 = 2.5, 2011 = 2, 2012 = 3. If the number of female students in 2011 and 2012 is equal, what is the ratio of male students in 2012 to male students in 2011?

A. 1 : 1
B. 2 : 1
C. 1.5 : 1
D. 2.5 : 1
questions.to_find: the ratio of male students in 2012 to male students in 2011

understand.plain: The line graph shows the ratio of male to female students over several years. The critical insight is that when the female count is the same in two years, the ratio of male students in those years equals the ratio of the male-to-female ratios for those years. This allows us to find the required ratio directly from the graph.

keywords.explain: R = male count / female count. If two years have the same female count F, then male count = R x F, so the ratio of male counts equals the ratio of R values.
keywords.example: If R_2011 = 2 and R_2012 = 3 and female count is equal, male 2012 / male 2011 = 3/2 = 1.5.

keywords.explain: The constraint that female count in 2011 = female count in 2012 simplifies the ratio of male counts to just the ratio of the graph values.
keywords.example: Female = F in both years; male 2012 = 3F, male 2011 = 2F, so 3F/2F = 3/2.

keywords.explain: The line graph gives the male-to-female ratio for each year directly. Identifying the correct year values is essential.
keywords.example: In year 2011, the graph reads 2.0; in 2012, the graph reads 3.0.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 380 220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='380' height='220' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' rx='8'/><line x1='55' y1='20' x2='55' y2='180' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='55' y1='180' x2='360' y2='180' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='55' y='196' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>2008</text><text x='115' y='196' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>2009</text><text x='175' y='196' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>2010</text><text x='250' y='196' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>2011</text><text x='330' y='196' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>2012</text><text x='30' y='180' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>1</text><text x='30' y='130' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>2</text><text x='30' y='80' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>2.5</text><text x='30' y='30' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>3</text><polyline points='55,30 115,130 175,80 250,130 330,30' fill='none' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='250' cy='130' r='6' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='330' cy='30' r='6' fill='var(--no,#D85A30)' stroke='var(--no,#D85A30)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='240' y='122' class='tag ok'>2</text><text x='320' y='22' class='tag no'>3</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Line graph of male-to-female ratio; year 2011 reads 2, year 2012 reads 3, highlighted in green and red respectively.

given.aim: Find male_2012 : male_2011 given female count is equal in 2011 and 2012.

terms.meaning: Male-to-female ratio in 2011 = 2 (from the line graph)
terms.example: If female count in 2011 = F, then male count in 2011 = 2F

terms.meaning: Male-to-female ratio in 2012 = 3 (from the line graph)
terms.example: If female count in 2012 = F (same as 2011), then male count in 2012 = 3F

terms.meaning: Female students in 2011 = Female students in 2012 = F
terms.example: This makes the ratio of males = ratio of their male-to-female ratios
terms.connects: the F cancels when dividing male 2012 by male 2011
directly derived from the graph values once the equal-female condition is used

given.plan: Let female count in both 2011 and 2012 be F. Express male counts using the graph ratios, then divide to get the required ratio.

steps.title: Express male counts in 2011 and 2012
steps.formula_raw: male count = ratio x female count
steps.apply: From the graph: R_2011 = 2, R_2012 = 3. Female count in both years = F (given equal). Male count in 2011 = 2F. Male count in 2012 = 3F.
steps.note: The equal female count is the pivotal condition that lets us compare male counts directly via the graph ratios.

steps.title: Compute the ratio male_2012 : male_2011
steps.note: The F cancels, confirming the ratio depends only on the graph values for those two years.

solution.result: The ratio of male students in 2012 to male students in 2011 is 1.5 : 1, which is option C.

questions.formula_note: This problem relies on reading the line graph correctly and applying the ratio relationship; no formula registry entry needed.

questions.question:
The following line graph shows f(x). Identify the correct expression for f(x) from the options below.

A. f(x) = -1 - |x - 1|
B. f(x) = -1 + |x - 1|
C. f(x) = -2 + |x - 1|
D. f(x) = -2 - |x - 1|
questions.to_find: the algebraic expression for f(x) that matches the given line graph

understand.plain: The graph shows a V-shaped function with its vertex (lowest point) at the point (1, -2). The two arms of the V slope upward with a slope of +1 on the right and -1 on the left. This is the signature of an absolute value function of the form f(x) = -2 + |x - 1|, which has vertex at (1, -2) and opens upward.

keywords.explain: f(x) = a + |x - h| has vertex at (h, a). The graph is V-shaped: it decreases with slope -1 for x < h and increases with slope +1 for x > h (for standard coefficient 1).
The minimum point of f(x) = a + |x - h| is at (h, a). Identifying the vertex from the graph directly gives both h and a.
keywords.example: If the lowest point is at x = 1, y = -2, then h = 1 and a = -2.

keywords.explain: A positive coefficient (+1) on |x - h| opens the V upward (minimum at vertex). A negative coefficient opens it downward (maximum at vertex).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 300 240'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='300' height='240' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' rx='8'/><line x1='150' y1='10' x2='150' y2='230' stroke='var(--mut,#5F5E5A)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='10' y1='120' x2='290' y2='120' stroke='var(--mut,#5F5E5A)' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='285' y='115' class='sub mut'>x</text><text x='155' y='18' class='sub mut'>y</text><text x='155' y='135' class='sub mut'>0</text><text x='200' y='135' class='sub mut'>1</text><text x='135' y='100' class='sub mut'>-1</text><text x='135' y='160' class='sub mut'>-2</text><line x1='200' y1='120' x2='200' y2='125' stroke='var(--mut,#5F5E5A)' stroke-width='1'/><polyline points='50,20 200,170 290,80' fill='none' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='3'/><circle cx='200' cy='170' r='6' fill='var(--no,#D85A30)' stroke='var(--no,#D85A30)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='208' y='178' class='tag no'>(1,-2)</text><text x='60' y='60' class='sub ink'>slope=-1</text><text x='220' y='60' class='sub ink'>slope=+1</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: V-shaped graph with vertex at (1, -2), left arm sloping downward and right arm sloping upward, representing f(x) = -2 + |x - 1|.

given.aim: Identify which of the four functions A-D matches the V-shaped graph with vertex at (1, -2) opening upward.

terms.meaning: The lowest point of the graph is at (1, -2)
terms.example: At x = 1: f(1) = -2. This is the minimum of the V-shape.
terms.connects: determines h = 1 and a = -2 in the general form f(x) = a + |x - h|

terms.meaning: The V opens upward, meaning the coefficient of |x - h| is positive (+1)
terms.example: For x > 1, f increases; for x < 1, f also increases as x moves away from 1
terms.connects: rules out options A and D which have negative leading sign -|x-1| (open downward)

terms.meaning: Option B: f(x) = -1 + |x-1|, vertex at (1,-1). Option C: f(x) = -2 + |x-1|, vertex at (1,-2)
terms.example: At x = 1: B gives f(1) = -1, C gives f(1) = -2. Graph vertex is at y = -2, so C is correct.
terms.connects: distinguishes between the two upward-opening candidates

terms.example: The graph passes through (-1, 0) and (3, 0): f(3) = -2 + 2 = 0.
terms.connects: verifying option C against the graph intercepts

given.plan: Read the vertex from the graph. Use vertex form f(x) = a + |x - h| to determine h and a. Verify the sign (upward vs downward opening) and match against the options.

steps.title: Identify key features from the graph
steps.apply: Vertex is at (1, -2): minimum point, so b > 0 (opens upward). The V-shape has slopes of magnitude 1, so b = 1. Thus f(x) = -2 + |x - 1|.
steps.note: Reading the vertex and direction of opening directly from the graph narrows the answer to a single option.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong options
steps.apply: A: f(1) = -1 - 0 = -1 (wrong, vertex y = -2). B: f(1) = -1 + 0 = -1 (wrong). C: f(1) = -2 + 0 = -2 (correct). D: f(1) = -2 - 0 = -2 but opens downward (wrong direction). Only C gives vertex (1,-2) opening upward.
steps.note: Plugging x = 1 quickly eliminates options A and B. Checking slope direction eliminates D.

steps.title: Verify option C with another point
steps.apply: At x = 3: f(3) = -2 + |3-1| = -2 + 2 = 0. At x = -1: f(-1) = -2 + |-1-1| = -2 + 2 = 0. The graph should pass through (3, 0) and (-1, 0), confirming x-intercepts at distance 2 from vertex.
steps.note: Checking additional points confirms option C fully matches the displayed graph.

solution.result: The correct expression is f(x) = -2 + |x - 1|, which is option C.

questions.formula_note: Absolute value function vertex form is not in the registry; the solution relies on direct reading of graph features and substitution.
